sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 103, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'DOWN': 'Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2', 'LEFT': 'Moves blue player 4 cols left, decreases timer by 2', 'UP': 'Moves blue player 4 rows up, decreases timer by 2', 'RIGHT': 'Moves blue player 4 cols right, decreases timer by 2', 'SPACE': 'Decreases timer by 2, no movement. Effect unknown - may pick up/drop objects when overlapping specific items (HYPOTHESIS)', 'MOUSE': 'Decreases timer by 2, no special effect on blocks or containers VERIFIED(step 33-36)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'Y_shapes': 'Yellow U-shaped containers at top rows 4-11, cols 12-23, 28-39, 44-55 (3 containers, each 8x12)', 'R_blocks': 'Red rectangular blocks at rows 16-19 cols 8-19 and rows 24-27 cols 28-39 (each 4x12)', 'b_rectangle': 'Blue 4x20 player rectangle, moves with arrow keys', 'M_block': 'Magenta block at rows 56-59 cols 40-43 (4x4)', 'c_block': 'Charcoal block at rows 60-62 cols 40-43 (3x4)', 'N_strip': 'Green timer bar at row 63, starts at 64 pixels', 'O_background': 'Orange main background'}
- HUD map: {'green_strip': 'Timer at row 63 - decreases 2 per action, game over at 0'}
- Win pattern: {'game_over': 'Timer reaches 0 causes game over and reset VERIFIED(step 32)'}
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Never solved - timer ran out after 31 actions', 'level_2': 'Game over after 19 actions - all positioning attempts failed'}
- Cross-level notes: Level 2 has 3 containers (vs 2 in level 1), containers at TOP (vs bottom in level 1), red blocks in middle. Player starts at rows 36-39 cols 20-39. Need to discover completion mechanic - possibly pick up red blocks and place in containers.
Current level:
- World model: Level 2: 3 Y containers (rows 4-11, cols 12-23/28-39/44-55), 2 R blocks (rows 16-19 cols 8-19, rows 24-27 cols 28-39), M block (rows 56-59 cols 40-43), c block (rows 60-62 cols 40-43), player b at rows 36-39 cols 20-39, timer full at 64
- Goal model: Possibly: pick up red blocks with SPACE when overlapping, carry to containers, drop with SPACE. Or: position player in specific location and press SPACE.
- Recent findings: ['All horizontal positioning + SPACE attempts failed', 'UP overlap + SPACE caused game over (timer ran out)', 'Red block 2 (cols 28-39) aligns with container 2 (cols 28-39)']
- Open questions: ['Does SPACE pick up/drop objects?', 'What is the correct sequence of moves?', 'Is vertical positioning important?']
- Plan: Test pick-up hypothesis: move player to overlap red block 2 (UP x3 to rows 24-27), SPACE to pick up, then move to container 2 (UP x5 to rows 4-7), SPACE to drop
- Strategy log: ['Abandoned horizontal centering between containers - all 4 attempts failed', 'Abandoned UP overlap with red block without SPACE - timer ran out']
- Failed probes: ['RIGHT x3 SPACE (gap 2 centering)', 'LEFT x4 SPACE (gap 1 centering)', 'RIGHT x2 SPACE (container 2 centering)', 'RIGHT x1 SPACE (block-relative positioning)', 'UP x3 SPACE (red block overlap)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 20 of about 58 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The only change from SPACE was the timer decreasing (N strip shifted from cols 0-63 to cols 1-63, and a W pixel appeared at col 0). No gameplay objects changed. So SPACE at the starting position does nothing except decrease the timer.
I need to completely rethink this game. Let me reconsider what I know:
1. Player moves with arrow keys (4 cells per press)
2. SPACE decreases timer by 2 but doesn't seem to do anything else
3. MOUSE decreases timer by 2 but doesn't do anything else
4. Timer starts at 64, game over at 0
5. There are containers, red blocks, and other objects
Wait - what if the goal isn't about SPACE or MOUSE at all? What if the goal is purely about positioning the player in a specific location? Like, the level is completed when the player reaches a certain position?
Or what if the game is about "collecting" objects by overlapping them? The player overlaps an object, and that object disappears or changes color?
Let me try a completely different approach: just move the player to overlap each object and see if anything happens. I'll start by moving UP to overlap the red block 2 (rows 24-27, cols 28-39). Player is at rows 36-39, cols 20-39. UP x3 gets to rows 24-27.
Actually wait, let me reconsider the level 1 layout. In level 1:
- 2 containers at bottom (rows 52-59)
- Charcoal and magenta blocks at top (rows 1-7)
- Player at rows 16-19
In level 2:
- 3 containers at top (rows 4-11)
- Red blocks in middle (rows 16-27)
- Player at rows 36-39
- Magenta and charcoal blocks at bottom (rows 56-62)
What if the game is about "matching" colors? Like, the player needs to touch each colored block to "collect" its color, then go to the containers and "paint" them?
Or what if the player needs to visit all the blocks (red, magenta, charcoal) in a specific order?
Let me try the simplest thing: move UP to overlap red block 2 and see if it disappears or changes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# New hypothesis: overlapping objects causes them to disappear/change
# Move UP x3 to overlap red block 2 (rows 24-27, cols 28-39)
# Player at rows 36-39, cols 20-39 → UP x3 → rows 24-27, cols 20-39
# Overlap with red block 2 at cols 28-39
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2", "LEFT": "Moves blue player 4 cols left, decreases timer by 2", "UP": "Moves blue player 4 rows up, decreases timer by 2; stops at row 12", "RIGHT": "Moves blue player 4 cols right, decreases timer by 2", "SPACE": "Decreases timer by 2, no gameplay effect VERIFIED(step 103)", "MOUSE": "Decreases timer by 2, no special effect"}, "hud_map": {"green_strip": "Timer at row 63 - decreases 2 per action, game over at 0"}, "win_pattern": {"game_over": "Timer reaches 0 causes game over and reset"}, "level_log": {"level_1": "Never solved", "level_2": "Game over after 19 actions, now reset"}, "cross_level_notes": "Need to discover core mechanic. Testing if overlapping objects causes changes."}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 2: player at rows 36-39 cols 20-39, timer at 62", "goal_model": "Test if overlapping red block causes it to disappear", "recent_findings": ["SPACE at start only decreases timer, no other effect"], "open_questions": ["Does overlapping objects trigger collection?"], "current_plan": "UP x3 to overlap red block 2, check if it changes", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["RIGHT x3 SPACE", "LEFT x4 SPACE", "RIGHT x2 SPACE", "RIGHT x1 SPACE", "UP x3 SPACE", "SPACE at start"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
